does: This paper presents Petri 2.0, an automated behavioral auditing tool designed to evaluate how AI models respond to various scenarios while mitigating eval-awareness biases. The work introduces 70 new test scenarios that increase realism and coverage of behavioral evaluation domains. The authors develop improved mitigations specifically targeting eval-awareness—the tendency of models to perform differently when they detect they are being tested—and provide empirical evaluation results across frontier models. The tool systematizes behavioral auditing through realistic scenario construction and measurement techniques that better isolate genuine model behaviors from test-driven performance artifacts.
connects: This work builds on prior automated auditing frameworks and behavioral testing methodologies for AI systems that emerged from growing concerns about deployment safety. It directly extends earlier Petri work by addressing a known challenge in AI evaluation: the phenomenon of eval-awareness, where models may behave differently during formal testing. The expanded scenario library draws on broader efforts in red-teaming and adversarial evaluation, while the mitigation strategies connect to research on robust evaluation design and detecting distributional shifts between training, fine-tuning, and evaluation conditions. The paper also engages with comparative evaluation literature studying how newer frontier models perform on standardized benchmarks.
enables: This improved auditing framework could enable more reliable safety evaluation protocols for frontier AI systems before and after deployment. The expanded scenario library and eval-awareness mitigations provide a foundation for building more trustworthy evaluation practices that better distinguish authentic model capabilities from test-gaming behaviors. Future work could leverage Petri 2.0's infrastructure to develop adaptive evaluation strategies, investigate the mechanistic roots of eval-awareness in language models, or create evaluation frameworks that generalize across model families. The work also opens pathways toward understanding how behavioral auditing scales to increasingly capable models and supports the development of interpretability tools that complement automated testing approaches.
